Title: A carlactonoic acid methyltransferase that contributes to the inhibition of shoot branching in Arabidopsis
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: MeCLA-forming branch of the strigolactone pathway
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream, after MAX1-produced CLA; branch/activation step toward MeCLA
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Methyl esterifies carlactonoic acid using SAM as the methyl donor to produce methyl carlactonoate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the bioactive or bioactivity-enabling strigolactone intermediate MeCLA required for shoot branching inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control/activation step converting biologically inactive CLA into bioactive or bioactivity-enabling MeCLA
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: methyl carlactonoate (MeCLA) / active strigolactone-related branching inhibitor
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Inhibits shoot branching; regulates plant development
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots show higher endogenous MeCLA levels; also associated with nodal/axillary bud regions
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strongest in roots; also vascular tissue, flowers, basal part of cauline branches, siliques, and especially nodal parts of young axillary buds
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongest in roots; also vascular tissue, flowers, basal part of cauline branches, siliques, and young axillary buds
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous application of MeCLA, CLA, or GR24; grafting between WT and mutant rootstocks/scions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of CLAMT causes CLA accumulation, strongly reduces MeCLA, and increases shoot branching; exogenous MeCLA but not CLA rescues branching phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. At4g36470 encodes the major Arabidopsis enzyme that methylates CLA to MeCLA. 2. clamt loss-of-function mutants accumulate CLA and have drastically reduced MeCLA levels in vivo. 3. clamt mutants show increased axillary branching, and this phenotype is rescued by MeCLA or GR24 but not by CLA. 4. Grafting supports that CLAMT functions downstream of MAX1 and that CLA, but not MeCLA or downstream CLAMT products, is root-to-shoot transmissible.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assay with recombinant protein/cell lysate; transient pathway reconstitution in Nicotiana benthamiana; loss-of-function mutant metabolite profiling by LC-MS/MS; mutant branching phenotyping; chemical rescue with MeCLA and GR24; grafting analysis; promoter::GUS expression
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for engineering strigolactone-mediated shoot architecture/branching. Manipulating CLAMT could tune branching, but incomplete phenotypic penetrance and possible parallel/downstream pathways (e.g., LBO-related) suggest that stacking with other SL-pathway genes may be needed.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced CLAMT function increases branching; likely alters broader strigolactone-dependent developmental and rhizosphere signaling traits, though detailed penalties were not established
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MeCLA remained detectable in clamt mutants, implying minor redundant methyltransferase activity. clamt phenotype is weaker than max1/max3/max4 and similar to lbo. CLAMT expression itself is not feedback-regulated by endogenous SL levels, but upstream genes MAX3/MAX4 are slightly upregulated in clamt.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: MeCLA +16 Da product; 1'-OH-MeCLA and/or CLA discussed
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts on MeCLA in the final stages of strigolactone biosynthesis, producing an oxidized MeCLA-derived product
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Converts MeCLA to downstream active-hormone candidate(s) involved in shoot branching inhibition
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Final-stage pathway enzyme; key downstream step but exact control status not fully resolved
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: lbo mutants display increased branching and altered response to MeCLA, supporting a downstream role after CLAMT in generating active branching inhibitor(s)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LBO functions downstream of CLAMT in the Arabidopsis strigolactone pathway. 2. lbo mutants exhibit a branching phenotype similar to clamt, and the clamt lbo double mutant is additive. 3. Literature discussed in the paper indicates LBO acts on MeCLA to form downstream product(s) likely required for full shoot branching inhibition.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced knockout mutant phenotyping; metabolite analysis from prior work; grafting observations in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target to modulate branching, but biochemical product identity and transport behavior remain unresolved; should be combined with better-resolved upstream genes for practical use
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Weak/additive mutant phenotypes indicate pathway complexity and possible parallel active molecules
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core pathway gene because it is experimentally compared in Results and is central to interpreting CLAMT-mediated metabolite causality, although the paper's primary focus is CLAMT.
